Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered because I work part-time and don't have a lot of resources to fall back on. I tried to squeeze in about 9 hours a week, usually in chunks whenever I could find time between shifts. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often try to do practice problems or work through examples rather than just reading. I'd download materials and study on the bus or during breaks at work since I couldn't always be online.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because work got crazy, and then I'd have to cram before deadlines. I tried to be consistent, but with 16 online courses to juggle and limited time, regular schedules just weren't realistic for me. Deadlines were really what drove when I studied most intensely.
Q3: Usually a deadline coming up or getting a notification about something new posted would get me to log in. Sometimes I'd check in just to see if there were any announcements or if peers had responded to discussion posts. My anxiety about missing something important kept me logging in pretty frequently, even if I couldn't always engage deeply with the content right then.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became automatic to check the LMS first thing in the morning and before bed. The peer support group I was part of helped with that because we'd remind each other about stuff. It became part of my routine like checking messages. Having that accountability with other students made it feel less overwhelming to stay connected.
Q5: The video lectures were huge for me because I could watch them multiple times and really understand the concepts kinesthetically by pausing and trying things out myself. I watched almost 268 minutes of video content because that format just clicks better for me than reading. The discussion boards were also helpful when I was confused because seeing how other students explained things gave me different perspectives.
Q6: I tended to skip over the supplementary readings and some of the optional articles. With limited internet at home sometimes, I had to prioritize what I downloaded and used. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I usually couldn't justify spending time on it when I had so many courses to manage.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to what I needed to know for assignments or exams. Practical exercises where I could actually do something rather than just read about it were always more engaging for me. If I could tell it would help me pass, I was all in, even if it was challenging.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't really afford time-wise. Required ones got my attention first because I knew they affected my grade directly. Sometimes I wished I could explore the optional stuff more, but between work and managing all my courses with limited resources, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a ton of studying offline. I'd download lecture slides, PDFs, and videos when I had good internet access, then study them on my phone during commutes or breaks at work. I also made handwritten notes and created my own practice problems to work through physically. A lot of my real learning happened away from any screen.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing downloaded materials or working through problems in a notebook, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also talk through concepts with my peer support group in person or over text, which was really valuable but invisible to the system. My attendance looks low, but I was often still engaged with the material in other ways.
Q11: Yeah, this was my standard approach actually. I'd access something once to download it, then spend way more time with it offline than I ever did online. Like I'd watch a video once on the LMS to download it, then replay it three or four times on my phone later. The system only saw that initial access, not all the real studying that came after.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially when my anxiety spiked around exam time or when I had multiple deadlines across different courses hitting at once. Financial stress from limited resources also made it hard to focus sometimes. But I've learned to push through those moments because giving up isn't an option for me.
Q13: For sure. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed with 16 courses, I'd use the LMS in a more surface-level way, just checking boxes rather than deeply engaging. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid them temporarily until I could get help from peers. Fatigue especially affected how much I actually absorbed versus just going through the motions.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be watching a video or scrolling through materials but my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about bills or just too tired to focus. I'd technically be logged in and active, but nothing was really sticking. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but I kept trying because I knew I needed to keep pushing forward.
Q15: I definitely postponed some discussion posts and readings that felt less urgent, even though I knew participating mattered. My anxiety made me avoid things that felt overwhelming until I absolutely had to face them. But I always came back to the important stuff eventually because I'm not someone who gives up easily, even when things get hard.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd ramp up activity significantly in the days before something was due, logging in more frequently and spending longer sessions working. Without deadlines, I might drift, but that pressure kept me accountable. It wasn't ideal, but it worked for getting my assignments completed with decent scores.
Q17: Getting feedback definitely motivated me to engage more, especially when it was positive or showed me exactly what to improve. After getting good scores on assignments, I felt more confident logging in and participating. But when feedback was delayed, I'd feel anxious and uncertain about whether I was on the right track, which sometimes made me hesitant to continue.
Q18: The course structure with clear deadlines and organized modules helped me plan around my chaotic schedule. Having everything laid out in the LMS made it easier to prioritize what needed attention first. The structure gave me a framework to work within, even when my attendance was inconsistent due to other life demands.
Q19: The video content and downloadable materials made engagement easier because they fit my learning style and my need to study offline. What made it harder was when activities required real-time participation or constant internet access, which wasn't always possible for me. Clear instructions on assignments helped a lot, while vague expectations increased my anxiety.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce work that meets the requirements. They might be watching videos, reading everything, trying to learn, but anxiety or confusion prevents them from completing submissions. Or maybe they're exploring but haven't figured out how to apply what they're learning yet.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. My attendance was low and I wasn't always visibly active, but I still passed because I was doing real work offline. Downloading materials once and studying them extensively, working through problems on paper, discussing with peers outside the platform. Low visible activity doesn't mean low actual effort.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, what you click on, and whether you submit things on time. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the peer conversations, and the mental effort happening away from the screen. They see the surface but not the depth of what a student is actually doing to learn.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through motions to get a grade. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, trying to connect concepts, and pushing through even when it's hard. For me, engagement is about persistence and genuine effort, even if that effort doesn't always show up in the data.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety while still pushing forward, or how my peer support group kept me going during tough times. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying downloaded materials on buses and during work breaks. Most importantly, it wouldn't show my resilience, the fact that despite limited resources and high anxiety, I found ways to succeed.
